skill.name: open-show
skill.description: Convert Markdown, Word (.docx), PDF, Text (.txt), local HTML or any URL into a single full-screen playable HTML slideshow.
skill.summary: A Python-based tool that transforms documents and web pages into self-contained HTML presentations with smooth animations, keyboard navigation, and zero external dependencies.
skill.author: lumacoder

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported 214 findings with a risk score of 100/100, but most are false positives. The 119 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' detections in README.md and SKILL.md are Markdown code block examples showing bash commands, not actual Ruby code execution. The 9 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings in README.md, SKILL.md, and scripts/openshow.py are false positives matching CSS template strings and embedded JavaScript, not cryptography. The critical heuristic findings about 'code execution + network + credential access' are false positives built on incorrect credential file detections that matched SVG attributes and CSS values in the HTML template. All documentation URL detections are expected for a project README. Real concerns: the script disables SSL certificate verification as a fallback (scripts/openshow.py:286), invokes subprocess to launch an external browser (scripts/openshow.py:936), and makes HTTP requests to user-supplied URLs. These are legitimate for the tool purpose but warrant user awareness.

medium_findings.title: SSL Certificate Verification Disabled
medium_findings.description: When the initial HTTPS request fails, the script retries with verify=False (scripts/openshow.py:286), disabling SSL certificate validation. This could expose the connection to man-in-the-middle attacks when fetching URLs or remote images from untrusted sources.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct evidence: requests.get called with verify=False parameter after initial request failure, with urllib3 warning suppression.

medium_findings.title: Subprocess Execution for Browser Launch
medium_findings.description: The script uses subprocess.run to execute the 'openclaw' command (scripts/openshow.py:936). The file URL passed to subprocess is derived from user-provided input, though it is a local file path rather than shell-interpreted content. Risk is moderate because the subprocess arguments are passed as a list (not a shell string), preventing command injection.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: subprocess.run called with a list of arguments, not shell=True. The file path is constructed internally from the output directory, not directly from user input.

low_findings.title: HTTP Requests to User-Supplied URLs
low_findings.description: The script fetches arbitrary user-supplied URLs via requests.get (scripts/openshow.py:78, 281). This is expected functionality for URL-to-slideshow conversion, but users should be aware that the script makes outbound network connections to convert web URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct evidence: requests.get called with user-provided URL in parse_url and _to_data_uri functions.

low_findings.title: File Read and Write Operations
low_findings.description: The script reads input files and writes output HTML files (scripts/openshow.py:926). The output directory is created if it does not exist. This is expected behavior for a document conversion tool.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct evidence: open() for reading input files and writing output HTML at user-specified paths.

low_findings.title: Archive Library Usage for DOCX Parsing
low_findings.description: The script uses zipfile to read embedded images from .docx files (scripts/openshow.py:235-237). This is standard practice for DOCX parsing and poses minimal risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct evidence: zipfile.ZipFile used to extract word/media/ contents from DOCX, which is the standard DOCX format.

dangerous_patterns.title: SSL Verification Bypass on Fallback
dangerous_patterns.description: The parse_url function disables SSL certificate verification when the initial HTTPS request fails. This fallback behavior silently accepts invalid certificates and suppresses urllib3 warnings, potentially exposing connections to man-in-the-middle attacks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: verify=False is explicitly set with warning suppression, confirming intentional SSL bypass.

content.user_title: Convert documents and web pages to HTML slideshows
content.value_statement: Creating presentations from documents requires copying content into slide software and formatting each page manually. This skill converts Markdown, Word, PDF, text, HTML files or URLs directly into a single self-contained HTML slideshow with smooth animations and responsive design.
actual_capabilities: Convert Markdown (.md), Word (.docx), PDF (.pdf), text (.txt), HTML (.html) files or web URLs into a single HTML slideshow
Automatically split content into slides based on headings, word count, and image density
Apply different layout templates (cover, text, list, split, image, closing) based on slide content
Generate self-contained HTML with all CSS and JavaScript inlined, no external CDN required
Support keyboard navigation, touch swipe, timer display, and fullscreen mode in the output
Convert local and remote images to inline data URIs for offline portability
limitations: Does not support PowerPoint (.pptx) files as input
Old Word format (.doc) is not supported and must be converted to .docx first
PDF conversion embeds pages as images, which can produce large output files
Complex web page layouts may not extract cleanly through DOM parsing

use_cases.title: Quick presentation from meeting notes
use_cases.description: Convert a Markdown document of meeting notes or project updates into a presentation-ready HTML slideshow without opening presentation software.

use_cases.title: Research paper to lecture slides
use_cases.description: Transform academic papers in PDF format into slide decks for classroom lectures or conference presentations.

use_cases.title: Web article into shareable deck
use_cases.description: Convert a blog post or online article URL into a self-contained slideshow that can be shared via a single HTML file.

prompt_templates.title: Basic document conversion
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert the file at [file path] into an HTML slideshow.
prompt_templates.scenario: User has a Markdown or text document they want to present.

prompt_templates.title: Web page to slides
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert the web page at [URL] into a slideshow and open it in the browser.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to present content from an online article.

prompt_templates.title: PDF presentation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a slideshow from the PDF at [file path] with the --open flag to preview immediately.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs to present a PDF report as slides.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-format batch conversion
prompt_templates.prompt: I have several documents in different formats (.md, .docx, .pdf). Convert each one into a separate HTML slideshow in the output directory [directory path].
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs to prepare multiple presentations from mixed document formats.

output_examples.input: A Markdown document about climate change with headings, paragraphs, and images
output: A single HTML file with a cover slide from the H1 heading
Content slides split by H2/H3 sections with appropriate layouts
Images converted to inline data URIs for offline viewing
Navigation via arrow keys, click zones, and touch swipe

output_examples.input: A web URL pointing to a technical blog post
output: A self-contained HTML slideshow with content extracted from the page body
Navigation elements, ads, and footers automatically removed
Remote images downloaded and inlined as data URIs
Built-in timer and fullscreen toggle for presentation mode

best_practices: Use clear heading levels (H1, H2, H3) in your source documents to control slide boundaries and hierarchy
Keep individual paragraphs under 300 characters for optimal slide readability, the tool will auto-split longer text
Test the generated HTML file in a browser before presenting to verify layout and image rendering
anti_patterns: Do not use the tool with PowerPoint (.pptx) files, this format is not supported and will fail
Avoid converting extremely large PDF files with hundreds of pages, the output HTML will be very large and slow to load
Do not rely on the tool for complex web pages with heavy JavaScript rendering, save the page as local HTML first for better results

faq.question: What file formats does this skill support as input?
faq.answer: It supports Markdown (.md, .markdown), Word (.docx), PDF (.pdf), plain text (.txt), HTML (.html, .htm) files and web URLs (http or https).

faq.question: Does the output HTML require an internet connection to display?
faq.answer: No. The generated HTML file is completely self-contained with all CSS, JavaScript, and images inlined. It works offline without any external dependencies.

faq.question: Can I use PowerPoint (.pptx) files as input?
faq.answer: No. The tool does not support .pptx format. You need to convert PowerPoint files to .docx or PDF first before using this skill.

faq.question: How does the tool decide where to split slides?
faq.answer: It uses heading levels (H1, H2, H3) as natural section boundaries, then splits further within sections if content exceeds 300 characters, 3 images, or 6 blocks per slide.

faq.question: What keyboard controls are available in the slideshow?
faq.answer: Arrow keys or Page Up/Down for navigation, F for fullscreen, T to show or hide the timer, Space for next slide. Mobile users can swipe left or right to change slides.

faq.question: Does the tool work with Chinese and other non-English languages?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill supports UTF-8 encoding and handles Chinese, Japanese, Korean, and other languages in input documents and output slideshows.
